messages.content: can template class be exported

As an AI language model, I do not have the capability to export code. However, in C++, template classes can be exported using the "export" keyword. This keyword allows the template code to be compiled separately and linked at runtime. However, the "export" keyword is not widely supported and has been removed in the latest version of the C++ standard. As an alternative, users can define their template classes in header files or create implementations for all types they plan to instantiate.
template class needs to instantiate in compile time ,   how can it be exported as dll export？
can i declare static variable in a header file, because my template class want to access it

`Yes, you can declare a static variable in a header file and access it in your template class. However, you need to be careful about doing so as it can lead to multiple definitions of the same variable if the header file is included in multiple source files.
`  i get unresolved external symbol error
child class constructor get error : Base is not initialized in this contructor.
Is there anything else you'd like me to help you with?
in c++ how do declare and  define a static variable to the cpp file.
`static CMyClass instance();` i have this code in cpp file, CMyClass contructor is not called, why?
`static CMyClass instance(); ` here i was not defining a function, i was defining a static variable in side the cpp file.
why can't i define a static variable like this `static CMyClass instance();`  why should i remove the `()`

In C++, when you define a static member variable, you do not need to include the parentheses after the variable name, because the parentheses are used to declare functions.

The reason you need to remove the parentheses in the declaration of a static member variable is because the parentheses indicate to the compiler that the declaration is that of a function, not a variable.

So, the correct syntax to define a static member variable in C++ is simply:

```
static CMyClass instance;
```

This declares a static member variable of type `CMyClass` without any parentheses.

Note that if you include the parentheses in the declaration, the compiler will interpret it as the declaration of a function that returns a `CMyClass` object and takes no arguments, not a static member variable. And if you define it this way, you will get a compilation error, because you cannot define a function without a body.

So, to define a static member variable, make sure to remove the parentheses from its declaration.
`static CMyClass static_instance("classTag_red");` what if the CMyClass' constructor takes a string argument. can i define a static variable `static_instance` like this?

so if the CMyClass takes argument in its constructor its fine to do `static CMyClass static_instance(“classTag_red”);` if the constructor don't take arguments `static CMyClass static_instance();` will be mistaken as a function not a definition for a variable
